**DETAILED ACTION**

**_Notice of Pre-AIA or AIA Status_**

The present application, filed on or after March 16, 2013, is being examined under the first inventor to file provisions of the AIA.

**_Continued Examination Under 37 CFR 1.114_**

A request for continued examination under 37 CFR 1.114, including the fee set forth in 37 CFR 1.17(e), was filed in this application after final rejection. Since this application is eligible for continued examination under 37 CFR 1.114, and the fee set forth in 37 CFR 1.17(e) has been timely paid, the finality of the previous Office action has been withdrawn pursuant to 37 CFR 1.114. Applicant's submission filed on 11/3/2025 has been entered.

**_Status of Prior Rejections/Response to Arguments_**

**_RE: Rejection of claims 1-8 and 20-31 under 35 USC 112(a):  
_**Cancellation of claims 20-31 renders the rejection thereof **moot**.

While Applicants have changed the wording of the limitations previously identified as new matter the same meanings/limitations are required.

Applicants have traversed the rejection as it applies to claims 1-8 on the grounds that the features are inherent in the use of dehydrated membrane and understood in the field.

Specifically, Applicants assert dehydration renders the biologically membrane substantially acellular. Applicants provide no support for this statement. This statement is contradicted by teachings in the art. See, for example, Daniel et al (US Patent 8709494). Daniel, claim 1, recites a dehydrated amniotic membrane which retains fibroblast cell layer. Dehydration does not necessarily result in decellularization.

Applicants further assert that decellularized amniotic membrane has no epithelial orientation. Applicants provide no support for this statement. Amniotic membrane has the following structure: (From Malhotra et al, World J Transplant, 2014):![](data:image/png;base64,)

Even upon decellularization, which would remove the epithelium and cellular layers of the stroma, the residual structure has a basement membrane face and a stromal matrix face. The basement membrane is still an ‘epithelial side’, despite absence of epithelium, and the stromal side is still ‘stromal side’, despite removal of cells. Thes basement membrane and stroma matrix are different, and thus there is natural ‘sidedness’, even to a decellularized amniotic membrane.

Applicants further assert that the specification provides inherent support that the method excludes corneal debridement or ‘corneal tissue removal’. Applicants assert that such removal of corneal tissue would undermine the purpose of the treatment (which is to improve corneal health prior to cataract surgery). This argument is not found persuasive. The original disclosure is silent as to whether corneal debridement is performed or not. Applicants cannot add an exclusory limitation without explicit support in the original disclosure, particularly when the limitation is introduced to differentiate from prior art.

The arguments are not found persuasive. The rejection is **maintained**.

**_RE: Rejection of claims 1-8 and 20-31 under 35 USC 112(b):_**

Cancellation of claims 20-31 renders the rejection thereof **moot**.

Applicants did not traverse the rejections, nor make any amendments to address the issues which were the basis of the rejections.

The rejections are **maintained**.

**_Declaration under 37 CFR 1.132_**

The declaration of Blake Smith has been received and entered into the application file.

As there is no prior art rejection at this time, the declaration is placed in the application file, but is not relevant to any rejection of record.

**_Maintained/Modified Grounds of Rejection_**

**_Claim Rejections - 35 USC § 112_**

The following is a quotation of the first paragraph of 35 U.S.C. 112(a):

(a) IN GENERAL.—The specification shall contain a written description of the invention, and of the manner and process of making and using it, in such full, clear, concise, and exact terms as to enable any person skilled in the art to which it pertains, or with which it is most nearly connected, to make and use the same, and shall set forth the best mode contemplated by the inventor or joint inventor of carrying out the invention.

The following is a quotation of the first paragraph of pre-AIA 35 U.S.C. 112:

The specification shall contain a written description of the invention, and of the manner and process of making and using it, in such full, clear, concise, and exact terms as to enable any person skilled in the art to which it pertains, or with which it is most nearly connected, to make and use the same, and shall set forth the best mode contemplated by the inventor of carrying out his invention.

**Claims 1-8 are rejected under 35 U.S.C. 112(a) or 35 U.S.C. 112 (pre-AIA), first paragraph, as failing to comply with the written description requirement.** The claim(s) contains subject matter which was not described in the specification in such a way as to reasonably convey to one skilled in the relevant art that the inventor or a joint inventor, or for applications subject to pre-AIA 35 U.S.C. 112, the inventor(s), at the time the application was filed, had possession of the claimed invention.

Regarding claim 1: The limitation at lines 7-11 requires that the dehydrated amniotic membrane is applied to the eye in a _non-invasive manner that avoids corneal tissue removal._ To find support in the original disclosure there must be explicit disclosure of the negative or exclusionary limitation, or at least positive recitation of an element in order to specifically exclude that element. In the instant case, the original disclosure makes no mention of corneal tissue removal (_i.e._ corneal debridement or corneal disruption), thus the amendment to exclude such an activity is not supported. Applicants’ argument (Pg 3-4 of Remarks) that the objective of the method is to improve corneal health, and such would be undermined by corneal debridement, and thus the exclusion of corneal debridement or disruption is inherently supported by the original disclosure is not persuasive. Corneal debridement is a common technique in healing corneal ulcers (See, e.g. Yeu et al). Furthermore, a lack of literal basis in the specification for a negative limitation may not be sufficient to establish a _prima facie_ case for lack of descriptive support. _Ex parte Parks,_ 30 USPQ2d 1234, 1236 (Bd. Pat. App. & Inter. 1993). See MPEP 2173.05(i).

Additionally, the new limitation at lines 15-16 that state the dehydrated form of the amniotic membrane _inherently lacks a defined epithelial orientation_ is new matter. As well as the limitation at lines 17-19 that the dehydrated amniotic membrane is placed on the ocular surface _without_ the need to determine or align an epithelial side relative to the cornea is new matter. Applicants’ argument (Pg 3 of Remarks) that a dehydrated amniotic membrane is acellular and orientation-independent is unsupported and inaccurate. There is no support in the current specification that the dehydrated amniotic membrane is decellularized. Dehydrated amniotic membrane tissue is not necessarily substantially acellular. In support see Daniel et al (US Patent 8709494), claim 1, which recites a dehydrated amniotic membrane which retains fibroblast cell layer. Furthermore, even if decellularized, the amniotic membrane does have distinct stromal vs basement membrane faces, and distinguishing between these two faces is important for correct usage and placement of the graft. In support see Malhotra et al (World J Transplant, 2014), at Pg 114 Malhotra et al explain that the basement membrane and stromal sides have different properties regarding cell growth and regulation of immune response. Thus, there is no literally or inherent basis to say that the dehydrated amniotic membrane is decellularized and lacks defined epithelial orientation. Furthermore, there is no mention (positive or negative) of detecting the orientation of the amniotic membrane prior to placement. The absence of a positive step of detecting orientation does not provide support for excluding a step of orientation.

All of claims 2-8 depend from claim 1, inherit the deficiency, and thus are rejected on same basis.

**_Claim Rejections - 35 USC § 112_**

The following is a quotation of 35 U.S.C. 112(b):

(b) CONCLUSION.—The specification shall conclude with one or more claims particularly pointing out and distinctly claiming the subject matter which the inventor or a joint inventor regards as the invention.

The following is a quotation of 35 U.S.C. 112 (pre-AIA), second paragraph:

The specification shall conclude with one or more claims particularly pointing out and distinctly claiming the subject matter which the applicant regards as his invention.

**Claims 1-8 are rejected under 35 U.S.C. 112(b) or 35 U.S.C. 112 (pre-AIA), second paragraph, as being indefinite for failing to particularly point out and distinctly claim the subject matter which the inventor or a joint inventor (or for applications subject to pre-AIA 35 U.S.C. 112, the applicant), regards as the invention.**

Regarding claim 1: Line 15 states “following placement”. It is unclear what is placed, the amniotic membrane or the intraocular lens. Clarification is required.

All of claims 2-8 depend from claim 1, inherit the deficiency, and thus are rejected on same basis.

Regarding claim 2: As amended, claim 1 requires a step of _preparing_ an amniotic membrane by verifying that it is sterile and properly sized for the patient’s eye prior to _applying_ the membrane to the eye. Thus in claim 2 it is unclear how the step of _applying_ the membrane to the eye further comprises a step of ensuring its sterility and sizing it for application. It was already deemed properly sized in the previous step, thus what action is required to actively size it in the subsequent step? Clarification is required.

Regarding claim 8: The phrase “maintaining said amniotic membrane is maintained on the eye for at least one week” renders the claim indefinite. Furthermore, the phrase “monitoring the patient’s recovery is monitored prior to …” renders the claim indefinite. Furthermore it is unclear if an active step of performing biometric measurements is required or not. The metes and bounds of the claim cannot be determined.

**_Conclusion_**

All claims are identical to or patentably indistinct from, or have unity of invention with claims in the application prior to the entry of the submission under 37 CFR 1.114 (that is, restriction (including a lack of unity of invention) would not be proper) and all claims could have been finally rejected on the grounds and art of record in the next Office action if they had been entered in the application prior to entry under 37 CFR 1.114. Accordingly, **THIS ACTION IS MADE FINAL** even though it is a first action after the filing of a request for continued examination and the submission under 37 CFR 1.114. See MPEP § 706.07(b). Applicant is reminded of the extension of time policy as set forth in 37 CFR 1.136(a).

A shortened statutory period for reply to this final action is set to expire THREE MONTHS from the mailing date of this action. In the event a first reply is filed within TWO MONTHS of the mailing date of this final action and the advisory action is not mailed until after the end of the THREE-MONTH shortened statutory period, then the shortened statutory period will expire on the date the advisory action is mailed, and any nonprovisional extension fee (37 CFR 1.17(a)) pursuant to 37 CFR 1.136(a) will be calculated from the mailing date of the advisory action. In no event, however, will the statutory period for reply expire later than SIX MONTHS from the mailing date of this final action.
